summary: Jeff Atwood argues that experienced software developers develop a deep hatred for software precisely because they understand how it's made. Using the example of frantically stopping his partner from installing bundled camera software, he illustrates how programmers know that most software is unnecessary, poorly written, and frustrating. He cites David Parnas on the quality problem in programming and argues that because most software is written by incompetent programmers (himself included), most software inherently sucks. The piece concludes that this hatred is actually a healthy sign of competence—a programmer who doesn't immediately identify their own code as the worst they've seen hasn't developed enough self-awareness yet.
key_points: Bundled software from hardware companies is typically unnecessary, poorly made, and written by programmers who don't prioritize quality
The software industry has a quality problem, not a quantity problem—one bad programmer creates two new jobs a year
Programmers hate software because they work at the 'sausage factory' and know how poorly it's made
The best software choice is often no software at all, or the minimum necessary from reputable sources
Self-critical awareness of one's own code quality is a marker of programming competence
money_quotes: Nobody hates software more than software developers.
One bad programmer can easily create two new jobs a year.
We work at the sausage factory, so we know how this stuff is made. And it is not pretty.
The best choice of software is often no software.
I don't look forward to installing new software. On the contrary, I dread it.
I know how hard it is to get it right.
You don't hate software enough yet.
key_insight: A programmer's hatred of software—especially their own—is not cynicism but a hard-won understanding of how difficult quality software is to achieve, making self-criticism a reliable marker of competence.
